examples.title: Generic Process Model Framework
examples.description: Basic structure applicable to iterative and incremental models
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ProcessModel(type, increments)
BEGIN
 IF type = "incremental" THEN
 FOR i FROM 1 TO increments DO
 REQUIREMENTS ← GatherRequirements()
 DESIGN ← CreateDesign(REQUIREMENTS)
 IMPLEMENT ← BuildModule(DESIGN)
 TEST ← VerifyModule(IMPLEMENT)
 DELIVER(IMPLEMENT)
 END FOR
 ELSIF type = "iterative" THEN
 WHILE NOT complete DO
 REQUIREMENTS ← RefineRequirements()
 DESIGN ← ImproveDesign(REQUIREMENTS)
 IMPLEMENT ← BuildSystem(DESIGN)
 TEST ← VerifySystem(IMPLEMENT)
 EVALUATE ← GetFeedback()
 END WHILE
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Shows basic structure for iterative and incremental approaches - iterative refines the entire system each cycle while incremental adds new modules

examples.title: Spiral Model Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates risk-driven iterative approach of Spiral Model
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SpiralModel()
BEGIN
 cycle ← 0
 risk_level ← HIGH
 product_version ← "concept"
 
 WHILE risk_level > ACCEPTABLE DO
 cycle ← cycle + 1
 
 // Planning Phase
 PLAN(cycle, product_version)
 
 // Risk Analysis Phase
 risks ← IDENTIFY_RISKS(product_version)
 IF risks EXISTS THEN
 risk_level ← ANALYZE_AND_MITIGATE(risks)
 ELSE
 risk_level ← LOW
 END IF
 
 // Engineering Phase
 IF risk_level <= ACCEPTABLE THEN
 product_version ← ENGINEER(product_version)
 END IF
 
 // Evaluation Phase
 CUSTOMER_REVIEW(product_version)
 IF customer_satisfied THEN
 risk_level ← ACCEPTABLE - 1 // Exit loop
 END IF
 
 END WHILE
 
 FINAL_DELIVERY(product_version)
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Each spiral cycle includes planning, risk analysis, engineering, and evaluation phases. The loop continues until risks are mitigated to acceptable levels.
examples.timeComplexity: O(c * r) where c=cycles, r=risk analysis complexity

examples.title: Prototype Model Process
examples.description: Shows iterative prototype development with user feedback
examples.code:
PROCEDURE PrototypeModel()
BEGIN
 quick_mockup ← TRUE
 
 WHILE user_satisfied = FALSE DO
 // Gather basic requirements quickly
 basic_requirements ← QUICK_REQUIREMENTS()
 
 // Build rapid prototype (not production quality)
 prototype ← RAPID_BUILD(basic_requirements)
 
 // Present to user for evaluation
 user_feedback ← PRESENT_AND_GET_FEEDBACK(prototype)
 
 // Refine requirements based on feedback
 basic_requirements ← REFINE(user_feedback)
 
 // Check if prototype meets needs
 IF user_approves(prototype) THEN
 // Develop production version
 final_system ← PRODUCTION_BUILD(basic_requirements)
 user_satisfied ← TRUE
 ELSE
 // Continue iteration with refined needs
 quick_mockup ← FALSE
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN final_system
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Starts with quick mockups and iteratively refines based on user feedback until approved, then builds production-quality system
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * p) where n=iterations, p=prototype complexity

examples.title: Scrum Sprint Framework
examples.description: Demonstrates Agile Scrum iterative sprint structure
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ScrumProcess()
BEGIN
 product_backlog ← PRIORITIZE(user_stories)
 
 WHILE product_backlog NOT EMPTY DO
 // Sprint Planning - select items for this sprint
 sprint_backlog ← SELECT_FOR_SPRINT(product_backlog, sprint_duration)
 
 // Sprint Execution - time-boxed iteration
 FOR each day IN sprint_duration DO
 daily_standup() // What done, what next, impediments
 development_work()
 
 IF impediments THEN
 remove_impediments()
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Sprint Review - demonstrate working increment
 sprint_review(sprint_backlog)
 
 // Sprint Retrospective - improve process
 retrospective()
 
 // Update backlog with new insights
 product_backlog ← REFINED(product_backlog)
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Shows Scrum framework with time-boxed sprints, daily standups, sprint review and retrospective for continuous improvement
examples.timeComplexity: O(s * d) where s=sprints, d=days per sprint
